content.text: The baseline current-state maturity audit is the first concrete deliverable of a GenAI maturity assessment programme. It establishes the organisation's starting position across the six maturity dimensions before any improvement investment is made. Without a baseline, you cannot demonstrate progress, prioritise investment rationally, or present a credible transformation narrative to the board. The audit runs over five weeks and produces two outputs: a scored baseline report and a prioritised 90-day action plan.

attrs.alt: Baseline current-state maturity audit process flow

content.text: The scoping phase defines the audit's boundaries and authority before any evidence is collected. Scope creep is the primary failure mode in maturity audits — a scope that keeps expanding delays the baseline and produces a report that covers everything superficially rather than the core dimensions deeply.
The audit charter is a one-page document signed by the ESC chair that specifies:
The six dimensions to be assessed (or a subset if this is a partial audit).
The dimension weights agreed with the ESC before scoring begins.
The evidence types that will be accepted as scoring inputs.
The independent reviewer — the person or team who validates provisional scores.
The stakeholder groups from whom interviews will be conducted.
The output format — baseline report structure and ESC presentation date.
The charter's most important clause is who has final say when provisional and independent reviewer scores conflict. Document it before the audit begins, not after the conflict arises.
A minimum stakeholder set for a credible baseline audit:
BU General Managers or AI leads
End-users (engineers or analysts who use AI tools daily)
Evidence collection is where the audit's quality is determined. Scores supported by documentary evidence and live system demonstrations are defensible; scores based on verbal assurances are not.
AI risk policy, data governance policy, acceptable use guidelines
Matches actual deployed systems (spot-check against live environment)
Platform architecture, data lineage diagrams, MLOps pipeline
Auditor observes operation; not a prepared demo environment
Data catalogue search, model registry, deployment dashboard
Exported from system of record, not manually curated spreadsheet
Uptime reports, deployment frequency, KPI actuals vs. targets
LMS export showing AI literacy completions, course catalogue

Interviews surface what the evidence cannot show: whether processes are actually followed, whether the culture matches the policy, and whether the BU experience of the CoE matches the CoE's self-description. Structured interview guides ensure comparability across sessions.

INTERVIEW_GUIDES = {
    "strategy_vision": [
        "Walk me through the AI roadmap — where does it live and when was it last updated?",
        "How does the AI roadmap connect to the corporate strategy planning cycle?",
        "Who can kill or deprioritise an AI use case, and how does that decision get made?",
        "What would a BU lead need to do to get a new AI initiative funded?",
    ],
    "data_infrastructure": [
        "Show me the data catalogue — how do data engineers discover available datasets?",
        "Walk me through how a model gets from training to production. What are the manual steps?",
        "What happens when model performance degrades in production? Who gets alerted and how?",
        "How are data quality issues detected and escalated?",
    ],
    "talent_culture": [
        "Describe the AI team structure. Who reports to whom?",
        "What AI training have non-technical senior managers completed in the past 12 months?",
        "If a BU product manager wanted to learn enough to work effectively with the CoE, what path would they take?",
        "Have you lost any AI engineers in the past year? Why did they leave?",
    ],
    "risk_governance": [
        "Show me the AI risk policy. When was it last reviewed?",
        "Walk me through what happens when a model incident is detected.",
        "What bias testing did you run before your last model deployment?",
        "Which of your AI systems fall under the EU AI Act High-Risk category?",
    ],
}

def print_guide(dimension: str) -> None:
    questions = INTERVIEW_GUIDES.get(dimension, [])
    print(f"Interview guide: {dimension}")
    for i, q in enumerate(questions, 1):
        print(f"  {i}. {q}")

print_guide("data_infrastructure")
# Interview guide: data_infrastructure
#   1. Show me the data catalogue ...
#   2. Walk me through how a model gets from training to production ...
#   ...

Interview notes are not attributed by individual in the final report — they are aggregated by stakeholder group (CoE, BU, Risk/Legal). This encourages candid responses and protects individuals who might otherwise soften negative feedback to avoid conflict with their own team's provisional scores.
Certain interview responses are immediate signals that a provisional score is likely inflated:
— followed by inability to locate or describe it. Policy existence without operational familiarity scores no higher than Level 2.
— BU leads who cannot describe how a deployment or data quality issue would affect them signals poor information flow; deduct on Delivery & Ops.
'We're planning to implement that next quarter'
— Future plans do not contribute to the baseline score. Score the current state.
— Concentrating knowledge in a single individual is a Level 1–2 indicator for Talent & Culture regardless of that individual's competence.

Provisional scores are set by the audit lead (typically the CoE Lead working with the PMO) after reviewing all evidence and interview notes. The independent reviewer then scores each dimension separately, without seeing the provisional scores, before the calibration session.
Average of the two scores (rounded down to nearest integer)
Lower score stands; calibration session required to document the discrepancy and the evidence that resolves it
Audit charter requires ESC chair involvement before finalisation; indicates fundamental disagreement about evidence quality

The calibration session is a structured 2-hour meeting where the auditor and independent reviewer walk through each dimension where scores differ. The session produces a written resolution note for each discrepancy — this note becomes part of the baseline report appendix.
Overall maturity score, top 3 strengths, top 3 gaps
Score per dimension with evidence summary and rationale
Prioritised list of gaps with estimated effort to close
Top 5 actions, each with owner, timeline, and success metric
Evidence register, interview guide, conflict resolution notes
The action plan must contain actions that are:
— 'Implement automated data quality gates in the ingest pipeline' not 'Improve data quality'.
— Each action has a success metric tied to the maturity rubric: 'This moves data_infrastructure from Level 2 to Level 3'.
— A named individual (not a team or department) is accountable for delivery.
— A specific completion date within the 90-day window.
— Scoped to what can be achieved with existing budget and headcount; stretch actions go into the 6-month plan.
Limit the 90-day action plan to five actions. A plan with twenty actions produces zero actions — teams deprioritise everything when nothing is forced to the top.
Integrating the Baseline with Programme Planning
The baseline report feeds directly into three downstream planning artefacts. Without this integration the audit becomes an isolated exercise rather than a programme input.
Gap analysis justifies spend per dimension; weakest dimensions get first-year priority
Low-scoring dimensions define what must be sourced externally vs. built internally
Talent & Culture gaps drive hiring and L&D investment; specific role shortfalls named
Risk & Governance gaps define which controls must be in place before Tier-1 models deploy
Delivery & Ops gaps constrain which use cases can scale safely in Year 1
A baseline that does not connect to these artefacts will be filed and forgotten. Assign a named owner for each integration point before the report is presented to the ESC.
Improvement targets must be set for each dimension at the time the baseline is accepted — not deferred to the next planning cycle. The target-setting process:
Identify the minimum maturity level required for the programme's Year 1 objectives (e.g., Delivery & Ops must reach Level 3 to support production deployment of three use cases).

Set a 12-month target for each dimension. Target = current score + realistic increment given budget and headcount. Historically, gaining 1 full level in a dimension requires focused investment over 6–12 months.
Identify the two highest-gap dimensions (target minus current is largest) and designate them as 'investment priority' dimensions for the first budget cycle.
Accept that not all dimensions will advance in Year 1. A balanced portfolio of dimension improvement is less effective than concentrated improvement on the binding constraints.

A common error is setting the same target level for all dimensions (e.g., 'achieve Level 3 everywhere by year end'). This distributes effort evenly rather than concentrating it on the weakest links. Uneven targets are correct.

The baseline assessment establishes zero. The first re-assessment determines whether investment is producing maturity improvement. Schedule the first re-assessment within six months of accepting the baseline:
Use the same evidence criteria and scoring rubric as the baseline — comparability depends on consistency.
Use the same independent reviewer if possible; if not, brief the new reviewer on the prior scoring rationale before they score independently.
Compare dimension by dimension against the baseline, not just against targets — a dimension that regressed (e.g., Risk & Governance after a CISO departure) is as important as one that improved.
Publish the delta report to the ESC with a written explanation for each dimension that moved more than 0.5 points in either direction.
The audit's credibility depends on the independence of the reviewer. Independence has two components: no reporting relationship to the CoE Lead, and no stake in the programme's continuation.
High — independent reporting line to CFO or board
Regulatory environments where audit independence is required; programme > 12 months
Moderate — independent of CoE but may have shared objectives
Mid-size programmes; partial audits focused on Risk & Governance dimension
High — no organisational conflict of interest
First baseline audit; programmes with history of optimistic self-assessment
Low to moderate — may have political incentives
Internal pilots or small programmes; supplement with at least one independent item
None — not acceptable for a formal baseline
Never — self-assessment is an input, not the final score

The independent reviewer's role is to validate, not to audit from scratch. They receive the evidence pack and provisional scores, score each dimension independently, and participate in the calibration session. They do not interview stakeholders themselves in the standard process — that would double the stakeholder burden and add four weeks to the timeline.

Some baseline findings will be uncomfortable for the sponsoring executive — a Risk & Governance score of 1 when the risk framework was presented to the board as mature, or a Talent & Culture score of 2 when the organisation announced an AI upskilling programme six months ago. Three principles govern how sensitive findings are handled:
— The score is presented with the evidence that determined it. The sponsor's prior communications are not in scope; only the current observable state is.
— Sensitive findings are discussed with the sponsor in the calibration session before the report goes to the ESC. This gives the sponsor context to respond, not opportunity to change the score.
No score changes without new evidence
— A sponsor who disputes a score must provide documentary evidence that contradicts the auditor's finding. Seniority and verbal assurance are not evidence.
Scores drift up 0.5–1.0 points above independent assessment
Mandate independent reviewer from the start; build into charter
Policies that exist on paper but not in practice score as Level 3+
Require live demonstration for all platform-related evidence
Audit takes 12 weeks instead of 5; loses executive attention
Hard scope in charter; defer out-of-scope dimensions to next cycle
Scores disputed 6 months later when the auditor has moved on
Every score must have a two-sentence written rationale in the report
Baseline becomes a historic document with no operational impact
90-day action plan is a required report section, not an optional appendix
The baseline current-state maturity audit runs over five weeks: scoping, evidence collection, stakeholder interviews, scoring and calibration, and report with action plan.

The audit charter — signed by the ESC chair — must specify dimension weights, evidence acceptance criteria, independent reviewer identity, and conflict resolution rules before evidence collection begins.
Evidence is accepted only when documented, dated, and demonstrable in live systems; verbal assurances and future-plan descriptions do not contribute to baseline scores.
Conflict resolution between provisional and independent scores follows a written rule: lower score stands when discrepancy is ≥ 2 levels; ESC involvement required at ≥ 3 levels.
The 90-day action plan is limited to five specific, owned, time-bound actions — each with a measurable maturity uplift as the success criterion.
